cards.text: What is negative feedback in the endocrine system?
cards.answer: Hormone at target level inhibits its own upstream stimulus. Example: high T3/T4 inhibits TRH (hypothalamus) and TSH (pituitary). This maintains hormone levels in a narrow range. Loss of feedback (e.g., autonomous tumour) causes hormone excess.

tip.tip: The thermostat principle of hormone regulation.

cards.text: What are the actions of insulin?
cards.answer: Anabolic hormone. Promotes: glucose uptake (GLUT-4 in muscle/fat), glycogenesis, lipogenesis, protein synthesis. Inhibits: gluconeogenesis, glycogenolysis, lipolysis, proteolysis. Deficiency causes diabetes: hyperglycaemia, glucosuria, ketoacidosis (Type 1).

tip.tip: The only hormone that lowers blood glucose.

cards.text: What are the zones of the adrenal cortex and their hormones?
cards.answer: Zona glomerulosa: mineralocorticoids (aldosterone) - salt. Zona fasciculata: glucocorticoids (cortisol) - sugar. Zona reticularis: androgens (DHEA) - sex. Mnemonic: GFR = Salt, Sugar, Sex (from outside in).

tip.tip: GFR from outside in: salt, sugar, sex.

cards.answer: PTH (parathyroid): increases Ca2+ by bone resorption, renal reabsorption, and activating vitamin D. Calcitonin (thyroid C cells): decreases Ca2+ (minor role). Vitamin D (1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol): increases intestinal Ca2+ absorption. Normal serum Ca2+: 9-11 mg/dL.

tip.tip: PTH raises calcium, calcitonin lowers it.

cards.text: What is the difference between Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes?
cards.answer: Type 1: autoimmune beta cell destruction, absolute insulin deficiency, young onset, ketosis-prone, needs insulin. Type 2: insulin resistance + relative deficiency, older onset (but increasing in young Indians), obesity-associated, managed with lifestyle + oral drugs initially.

tip.tip: Type 1 = no insulin, Type 2 = insulin doesn't work well.

cards.text: What are the actions of growth hormone?
cards.answer: Direct: lipolysis, anti-insulin (diabetogenic), protein synthesis. Indirect (via IGF-1 from liver): linear growth, cartilage growth, organ growth. Excess: gigantism (children), acromegaly (adults). Deficiency: dwarfism (children). Pulsatile secretion, peaks during sleep.

Primary adrenal insufficiency: destruction of adrenal cortex (autoimmune, TB in India). Deficiency of cortisol + aldosterone + androgens. Features: hyperpigmentation (high ACTH), hypotension, hyperkalaemia, hyponatraemia, fatigue. Addisonian crisis: life-threatening, needs IV hydrocortisone.

cards.text: How does the posterior pituitary work?
cards.answer: Does not synthesise hormones - stores and releases ADH and oxytocin made in hypothalamus (supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei). ADH: water reabsorption in collecting duct (via aquaporin-2). Oxytocin: uterine contraction, milk ejection. Released by neural stimuli.
